data.title: Federal Reserve Flags a Potential Crisis in Home Equity Lending — and It Could Affect You
data.description:
A worrying report from the Federal Reserve said household debt and delinquencies were on the rise, with potential implications for all homeowners.

With the headline figure being a rise in total household debt of $191 billion in Q4 2025 to $18.8 trillion, the Federal Reserve Bank of New York's Center for Microeconomic Data delivered a sobering Quarterly Report on Household Debt and Credit 

While household debt levels were seen growing modestly to that level, according to the Fed, there were underlying concerns around increased delinquencies in lower-income areas and in areas with declining home prices. That could be an area of concern for those with a home equity line of credit (HELOC) mortgage, where lending has been on an uptrend since 2022.

Analysts explained some of the key reasons for the uptick in bad debt.

Delinquency Rates Hit Highs of 10 Years Ago

The Fed shared additional findings on its Liberty Economics blog, with those analysts pointing to rising unemployment as a key problem.

"Delinquency rates for mortgages are near historically normal levels, but the deterioration is concentrated in lower-income areas and in areas with declining home prices," said Wilbert van der Klaauw, a research advisor at the New York Fed.

"Counties experiencing the steepest increases in unemployment saw a notable worsening in mortgage delinquency by nearly 0.6 percentage points over the past year," the report said.

The data showed that delinquency rates in the lowest-income ZIP codes had risen to levels not seen since 2016, with aggregate delinquency rates highlighting that 4.8% of total debt was underwater. Total mortgage loans that were 90 days or more in delinquency were up from 1.09% in Q4 2024 to 1.3%.

For homeowners who are not yet delinquent but are feeling the squeeze, the data presents a complicated picture. 

U.S. homeowners collectively hold more equity than at almost any point in recent history, according to the Fed, and many are already tapping it. HELOC balances rose another $12 billion in Q4 2025 according to the New York Fed, continuing a 15-quarter climb that began as inflation pushed household expenses higher. 

But home equity lines typically carry variable rates, which means any reversal in Fed policy driven by renewed inflation from higher oil prices would push those monthly payments higher at exactly the moment household budgets are already under strain.

No Jobs and Higher Gas Prices Threaten the ‘Dual Mandate'

Fed policymakers regularly remind market participants of their ‘dual mandate' of price stability and maximum employment.

While there has been a recent slowdown in job creation, the bank faces big problems with price stability as gas prices rocket higher due to the conflict in Iran. Alongside potential disruptions to supply chains, officials may find it hard to keep inflation in check, with higher prices at the pump adding to further strain in lower-income areas of the country.

Further geopolitical stress comes at a bad time after Fed Chair Jerome Powell said recently that the country has seen no economic growth and no net private-sector job creation over the past several months.

The latest developments are a major blow for homeowners after a drop in interest rates from 4.5% at the onset of 2025 to the current level of 3.75%. Economists had been pricing in further cuts that would have eased the payment burden on millions of households, but a recent deterioration in the economy and the threat of another inflation fight could see rates reverse back up.

For a homeowner with a variable-rate HELOC, that is a direct and immediate cost increase.

What it Means for You

While HELOC loans are not currently seeing delinquency rates on the rise, there are signs that homeowners have been drawing down equity since the inflationary surge post 2022. Rising costs on variable-rate mortgage payments could add further upside to household debt numbers, but also have an impact on home sales.

The Center for American Progress, a left-leaning think tank, said recently that an increase in mortgage rates since the start of the Iran conflict would already cost the average first-time buyer with a 30-year deal an extra $756 per year, or an additional $22,800 over the life of the loan.

For homeowners weighing whether to tap equity to manage debt or cover rising expenses, the fixed versus variable rate distinction matters more now than it has in years. 

With the average homeowner sitting on roughly $295,000 in tappable equity, according to Cotality's Q4 2025 data, the opportunity is real, but so is the risk of acting without a clear understanding of how each product behaves when rates move. 

Image: Shutterstock

data.title: What's Going On With Hycroft Mining Stock Friday?
data.description:
Gold rose back above $4,400 per ounce after a sharp decline on Thursday, according to Trading Economics.

The Nasdaq is down 1.01% while the S&P 500 has shed 0.79%.

Trump Shifts Iran Deadline

Market sentiment shifted as President Donald Trump pushed back a critical deadline. Iran now has until April 6 to secure a deal to end the war.

This 10-day extension halted planned strikes against Iranian energy infrastructure. Trump asserted that negotiations were progressing “very well.”

Tehran Rejects U.S. Proposal

Despite the extension, friction remains high. Tehran reportedly rejected a 15-point U.S. proposal conveyed through Pakistan. A senior Iranian official told Reuters the deal served only American and Israeli interests. This geopolitical uncertainty often drives investors toward safe-haven assets like gold and silver.

Hycroft Operations in Focus

Hycroft operates as a gold and silver exploration-stage issuer. The company focuses on developing its flagship Hycroft Mine. This property is located northwest of Winnemucca, Nevada.

Technical Analysis

HYMC is trading 16.7% below its 20-day simple moving average (SMA) and 18.3% above its 100-day SMA, a split that suggests short-term pressure inside a still-elevated longer-term trend. Shares are up 852.01% over the past 12 months and are currently positioned closer to their 52-week highs than lows.

HYMC Stock Price Activity: Hycroft Mining Holding shares were up 6.54% at $33.22 at the time of publication on Friday, according to Benzinga Pro data.

Photo by Dodi Dharmanto via Shutterstock

data.title: Progress Software Likely To Report Higher Q1 Earnings; These Most Accurate Analysts Revise Forecasts Ahead Of Earnings Call
data.description:
Progress Software Corporation (NASDAQ:PRGS) will release earnings for its first quarter after the closing bell on Monday, March 30.

Analysts expect the Burlington, Massachusetts-based company to report quarterly earnings of $1.57 per share, up from $1.31 per share in the year-ago period. The consensus estimate for Progress Software's quarterly revenue is $246.4 million (it reported $238.01 million last year), according to Benzinga Pro.

On Jan. 20, Progress Software reported better-than-expected fourth-quarter adjusted EPS results and issued first-quarter guidance above estimates.

Progress Software shares fell 4.1% to trade at $27.98 on Friday.

Benzinga readers can access the latest analyst ratings on the Analyst Stock Ratings page. Readers can sort by stock ticker, company name, analyst firm, rating change or other variables.

data.title: Stock Of The Day: Will AT&T Break Out?
data.description:
As you can see from the chart below, AT&T was in an uptrend in August. But the trend ended around the $29 level. A selloff followed.

When this happened, some of the investors and traders who bought shares around $29 began to lose money. At this point, they decided that their decision to buy was a bad one.

They also decided to exit at breakeven if it was eventually possible.

As a result, when the shares returned to $29 in September, these unhappy investors and traders were able to bail out at breakeven. Their sell orders created resistance at the level once again.

A similar dynamic occurred in February and March.

Remorseful buyers who purchased shares previously at $29 came into the market as sellers. This put a ceiling on the price once again.

Many times, after reaching resistance, a stock will reverse and head lower.

This happens when anxious and impatient sellers start to undercut each other so the buyers will come to them. This can turn into a snowball effect that pushes the price lower.

If AT&T can get and hold above $29, it could be a bullish dynamic. It would mean that the large concentration of people who created the resistance with their sell orders is gone. New buyers must pay premiums to draw sellers back in.

This is a ‘breakout', and it may be about to happen with AT&T.

The ex-dividend date is the day on which a stock begins trading without the value of its next scheduled dividend payment. To receive a dividend, you must purchase the stock before this date. It is typically set one business day before the record date.

Image: Shutterstock

data.title: Nvidia Stock Just Became The Bargain Of The AI Boom
data.description:
Nvidia's current valuation metrics suggest the stock looks surprisingly reasonable by its own historic standards, despite standing at the foundation of the AI revolution.  

  • NVDA stock is down. See the chart and price action here. 

NVDA is a Bargain

Nvidia stock currently trades at roughly 34.95 times earnings and 19.44 times sales, per Benzinga Pro data, placing it near the lowest multiples seen since the early phase of the AI boom. 

For comparison, these ratios were briefly touched last April during the U.S.-China tariff turmoil — a period many investors feared would curb chip exports. 

The market's anxiety proved short-lived: Nvidia's shares subsequently staged a powerful rally that more than doubled its market capitalization to above $5 trillion by October, cementing its position as the world's most valuable technology producer.

The current backdrop feels strikingly similar but arguably stronger. Over the past year, Nvidia's revenue has soared 65%, reflecting relentless demand for its high-end GPUs that power data centers, large language models and cloud AI services. 

The company's latest financials continue to show expanding margins and rising free cash flow, suggesting its AI dominance is still deepening while competitors struggle just to secure chip supply.

At a P/E below 35, Nvidia is trading at a multiple comparable to far slower-growing tech peers — even though its top-line growth rate dwarfs almost every company in the S&P 500. 

That combination of rapid expansion and moderating valuation is what makes Nvidia's current setup particularly compelling. 

In effect, investors are paying a growth-moderate price for a hypergrowth business that remains at the center of the most powerful technology cycle of the decade.

With AI infrastructure demand set to accelerate further and Nvidia's next-generation Rubin chips expected to drive another wave of data center spending, the stock's recent pullback may represent not a warning sign, but another golden entry point in the defining trade of the AI era.

NVDA Price Action: According to data from Benzinga Pro, Nvidia stock was down 1.97% at $167.86 at the time of publication Friday.

Photo: Sudarsan Thobias / Shutterstock
